topic: Strings - String Replacement and Case Conversion

turns.content: Hey, I'm reviewing my colleague's code. They're replacing strings using replace() and converting cases with to_uppercase() and to_lowercase(). But wait, isn't that just like JavaScript string methods? That feels a bit awkward in Rust.

Yes, from a conceptual standpoint, the methods are similar because they both operate on UTF-8 strings. However, in Rust, it's more explicit — you use `.replace()` and `.to_uppercase()`, which are part of the `String` type itself, rather than just generic JavaScript functions.

Right, so to replace a substring, they're using `.replace(&self, &str, &str)`. That's straightforward. But what about if I want to replace multiple occurrences? Should I loop with `replace()` or is there a better way?

Good question! For replacing all occurrences of a substring, you can use `.replacen(&self, &str, usize)` which replaces up to a certain count. But if you want to replace all occurrences without limit, just calling `.replace()` is fine.
Oh, I see. So if I have a big string and want maximum performance for replacements, should I split by the substring and collect again? That sounds expensive.

That's one way to do it — splitting and collecting is definitely an option! But in Rust, you can also use regex-based replacements with `regex::Regex` if your case is more complex. For simple cases though, `.replace()` or `.replacen()` tend to be simpler.
Right, but wait — what about ASCII-only strings? Would I use the same methods? Like, would `to_ascii_uppercase()` and `to_ascii_lowercase()` work with UTF-8?

Good catch! Those functions are more for ASCII strings. For Unicode characters outside of ASCII, it won't change their case. So if you're sure your string is only ASCII, then yes — those methods will give you expected results.
